title: Mortgage Refinancing and the Concentration of Mortgage Coupons

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This New York Fed Current Issues in Economics and Finance article (March 1999 press release) examines why mortgage refinancing activity surged in 1998, finding that the concentration of outstanding mortgages in the 7.25-8.75% coupon range created a large refinancing wave when market rates dropped toward 7%. The authors identify two additional factors: low unemployment boosting borrower creditworthiness and mortgage 'seasoning' (2-5 year old loans being most likely to refinance).
key_points: By end of 1997, an estimated 83% of mortgage loans were concentrated in the 7.25-8.75% interest rate range
When 30-year fixed mortgage rates dropped toward 7% in January 1998, the spread to outstanding mortgages widened enough to make refinancing economically advantageous
Authors Paul Bennett, Frank Keane, and Patricia Mosser attribute the refinancing surge to this concentrated 'coupon cluster' becoming cost-effective to refinance all at once
Low unemployment rate boosted refinancing activity by building borrower creditworthiness and lender confidence
Mortgages aged 2-5 years ('moderately seasoned') were more likely to be refinanced than new or fully seasoned loans
Authors are from the New York Fed's Research and Market Analysis Group, Capital Markets Function
Published as part of the Current Issues in Economics and Finance series
why_it_matters: Understanding the concentration of mortgage coupons helps explain refinancing sensitivity to rate changes. This research documents how a clustered distribution of outstanding loans can create outsized refinancing waves when rates cross threshold levels, relevant to MBS prepayments and mortgage-backed securities valuation.
market_structure_context: This research examines mortgage market structure through the lens of coupon distribution and refinancing behavior, which affects MBS prepayment risk and mortgage-backed securities valuation. However, it does not address trading infrastructure, settlement, or market operations.
important_numbers: 83% of mortgage loans in 7.25-8.75% range by end 1997
7% threshold rate for refinancing in January 1998
